groups.title: Essentials
groups.description: Minimum information needed to understand the sequence, audience, goal, sender, CTA, language, and permission context.

groups.title: Audience & Journey
groups.description: Audience awareness, relationship stage, objections, trigger context, and legitimate personalization.

groups.title: Offer, Proof & Conversion
groups.description: Benefits, details, terms, pricing, evidence, quotes, deadlines, and relevant alternatives.

groups.title: Brand & Delivery
groups.description: Tone, sender perspective, email length, formatting, cadence, timing constraints, and emoji use.

groups.title: Compliance & Output
groups.description: Regulated topics, jurisdiction, restrictions, output depth, plain-text versions, and final requirements.

variables.label: Sequence name
variables.hint: Give the sequence a clear internal name.

help.what: Provide the sequence name for this email sequence.
help.why: It identifies the sequence and provides a useful heading for the generated package.
help.example: Good example: New customer onboarding
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, invented facts, or values that conflict with the actual sequence context.

variables.label: Sequence type
variables.hint: Choose the journey that most closely matches your goal.

help.what: Provide the sequence type for this email sequence.
help.why: Different sequence types require different progression, timing, and promotional intensity.
help.example: Good example: Lead nurture
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, invented facts, or values that conflict with the actual sequence context.

variables.label: Primary goal
variables.hint: Choose the single most important result the sequence should support.

help.what: Provide the primary goal for this email sequence.
help.why: The goal determines the sequence progression and primary CTA.
help.example: Good example: Build trust
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, invented facts, or values that conflict with the actual sequence context.

variables.label: Business or brand
variables.hint: Enter the sender’s business, product, organization, or brand name.

help.what: Provide the business or brand for this email sequence.
help.why: The model must know who is communicating and how to identify the sender.
help.example: Good example: Acme Studio
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, invented facts, or values that conflict with the actual sequence context.

variables.label: Offer or topic
variables.placeholder: Describe the product, service, event, resource, or topic.
variables.hint: Explain what the sequence is about and what the reader is being introduced to.

help.what: Provide the offer or topic for this email sequence.
help.why: The central offer or topic cannot be safely inferred.
help.example: Good example: Describe the product, service, event, resource, or topic.
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, invented facts, or values that conflict with the actual sequence context.

variables.label: Target audience
variables.placeholder: Independent designers considering their first client-management tool.
variables.hint: Describe the intended recipients and any important shared characteristics.

help.what: Provide the target audience for this email sequence.
help.why: Audience context materially changes the language, assumptions, objections, and CTA.
help.example: Good example: Independent designers considering their first client-management tool.
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, invented facts, or values that conflict with the actual sequence context.

variables.label: Key message
variables.placeholder: The main idea readers should understand after completing the sequence.
variables.hint: State the central takeaway in plain language.

help.what: Provide the key message for this email sequence.
help.why: It prevents the sequence from becoming unfocused or internally inconsistent.
help.example: Good example: The main idea readers should understand after completing the sequence.
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, invented facts, or values that conflict with the actual sequence context.

variables.label: Output language
variables.hint: Enter any language or regional language variant.

help.what: Provide the output language for this email sequence.
help.why: The language cannot always be inferred from the surrounding interface.
help.example: Good example: English
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, invented facts, or values that conflict with the actual sequence context.

variables.label: Number of emails
variables.hint: Choose between 2 and 10 emails.

help.what: Optional context for the number of emails field.
help.why: The required sequence length materially changes its structure and pacing.
help.example: Good example: 5
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Sequence depth
variables.hint: Controls how much strategic and educational detail is distributed across the sequence.

help.what: Optional context for the sequence depth field.
help.why: It allows the user to control complexity without specifying specialist copywriting rules.
help.example: Good example: Select depth
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Sender name
variables.hint: Add the person or team name that should appear in the sign-off.

help.what: Optional context for the sender name field.
help.why: A supplied sender identity makes the emails more natural and implementation-ready.
help.example: Good example: Alex from Acme
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Primary CTA
variables.hint: Choose the main action or let the model select a suitable low-friction step.

help.what: Optional context for the primary cta field.
help.why: The main conversion action determines how the sequence progresses.
help.example: Good example: Visit page
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: CTA destination URL
variables.hint: Provide the real destination for click-based CTAs.

help.what: Optional context for the cta destination url field.
help.why: The system must not invent links or send readers to an unspecified destination.
help.example: Good example: https://example.com/product
help.avoid: Avoid fake links, tracking links you cannot verify, or unrelated destinations.

variables.label: Contact permission
variables.hint: Describe why these recipients may lawfully and reasonably receive the sequence.

help.what: Provide the contact permission for this email sequence.
help.why: Permission and relationship context materially affect tone, frequency, promotion, and opt-out handling.
help.example: Good example: Confirmed subscribers
help.avoid: Avoid vague placeholders, invented facts, or values that conflict with the actual sequence context.

variables.label: Audience awareness
variables.hint: Choose how familiar recipients are with the problem and offer.

help.what: Optional context for the audience awareness field.
help.why: Awareness level changes how much explanation is needed before presenting the offer.
help.example: Good example: Problem aware
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Audience problem
variables.placeholder: What problem, frustration, or unmet need brings readers to this topic?
variables.hint: Focus on the most relevant problem rather than every possible pain point.

help.what: Optional context for the audience problem field.
help.why: A specific problem creates relevance and helps avoid generic copy.
help.example: Good example: What problem, frustration, or unmet need brings readers to this topic?
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Desired reader outcome
variables.placeholder: What useful change or result does the reader want?
variables.hint: Describe the realistic outcome the audience hopes to achieve.

help.what: Optional context for the desired reader outcome field.
help.why: It helps connect benefits and CTAs to a meaningful reader goal.
help.example: Good example: What useful change or result does the reader want?
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Relationship stage
variables.hint: Choose the audience’s current relationship with the sender.

help.what: Optional context for the relationship stage field.
help.why: The model should not assume trust or familiarity that does not exist.
help.example: Good example: Warm lead
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Sequence trigger
variables.placeholder: Signed up, left checkout, attended an event, or stopped using the product.
variables.hint: Describe the real action or event that starts the sequence.

help.what: Optional context for the sequence trigger field.
help.why: Triggered sequences must accurately acknowledge the real event without fabricating behavior.
help.example: Good example: Signed up, left checkout, attended an event, or stopped using the product.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Common objections
variables.hint: Choose only the barriers that genuinely affect this audience. Current form controls do not support checkboxes for this template yet, so list the relevant options separated by commas.

help.what: List one or more relevant options separated by commas. This preserves the prompt variable while using the current textarea control.
help.why: Relevant objections help give later emails distinct roles.
help.example: Good example: Price or budget, Trust or credibility
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Personalization data
variables.placeholder: Approved fields such as first name, plan, industry, or signup source.
variables.hint: Include only legitimate data that may safely be used in the emails.

help.what: Optional context for the personalization data field.
help.why: The system must distinguish real personalization from fabricated familiarity.
help.example: Good example: Approved fields such as first name, plan, industry, or signup source.
help.avoid: Avoid private, sensitive, or inferred data that the email system cannot legitimately use.

variables.label: Key benefits
variables.placeholder: List the most important practical benefits for this audience.
variables.hint: Describe useful outcomes rather than unsupported hype.

help.what: Optional context for the key benefits field.
help.why: Supplied benefits make the sequence more specific and commercially relevant.
help.example: Good example: List the most important practical benefits for this audience.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Important details
variables.placeholder: Features, process, eligibility, delivery method, dates, or requirements.
variables.hint: Add factual information readers need before taking action.

help.what: Optional context for the important details field.
help.why: Important operational facts cannot be safely invented.
help.example: Good example: Features, process, eligibility, delivery method, dates, or requirements.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Offer terms
variables.placeholder: Trial conditions, included services, refund terms, or purchase conditions.
variables.hint: Include exact terms that must be communicated accurately.

help.what: Optional context for the offer terms field.
help.why: Terms affect conversion copy and must not be inferred.
help.example: Good example: Trial conditions, included services, refund terms, or purchase conditions.
help.avoid: Avoid approximate commercial terms when exact wording, eligibility, or pricing matters.

variables.label: Pricing details
variables.placeholder: Price, billing period, plan differences, or approved discount.
variables.hint: Leave blank when pricing should not appear.

help.what: Optional context for the pricing details field.
help.why: Prices and discounts must only be used when supplied.
help.example: Good example: Price, billing period, plan differences, or approved discount.
help.avoid: Avoid approximate commercial terms when exact wording, eligibility, or pricing matters.

variables.label: Approved proof points
variables.placeholder: Verified metrics, certifications, research, customer numbers, or results.
variables.hint: Include only evidence that may be used publicly.

help.what: Optional context for the approved proof points field.
help.why: It allows credible support while preventing fabricated evidence.
help.example: Good example: Verified metrics, certifications, research, customer numbers, or results.
help.avoid: Avoid invented metrics, paraphrased testimonials, or evidence that has not been approved for public use.

variables.label: Approved quotes
variables.placeholder: Paste exact approved testimonials or quotations with attribution.
variables.hint: Do not summarize unapproved feedback as a testimonial.

help.what: Optional context for the approved quotes field.
help.why: Exact source wording is required to use testimonials truthfully.
help.example: Good example: Paste exact approved testimonials or quotations with attribution.
help.avoid: Avoid invented metrics, paraphrased testimonials, or evidence that has not been approved for public use.

variables.label: Deadline or availability
variables.hint: Add only real deadlines, availability limits, or launch dates.

help.what: Optional context for the deadline or availability field.
help.why: It prevents the model from inventing urgency or scarcity.
help.example: Good example: Registration closes 18 September at 17:00 CET.
help.avoid: Avoid fake urgency, artificial scarcity, or deadlines that are not real.

variables.label: Alternatives to address
variables.placeholder: Describe relevant alternatives and factual differences.
variables.hint: Use this for fair positioning, not unsupported superiority claims.

help.what: Optional context for the alternatives to address field.
help.why: Supplied comparison context can improve positioning without encouraging disparagement.
help.example: Good example: Describe relevant alternatives and factual differences.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Tone
variables.hint: Choose the overall communication style.

help.what: Optional context for the tone field.
help.why: Tone is a legitimate brand preference that cannot always be inferred.
help.example: Good example: Clear and natural
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Brand voice notes
variables.placeholder: Calm, practical, slightly informal, and never overly enthusiastic.
variables.hint: Describe distinctive voice traits or paste a short approved example.

help.what: Optional context for the brand voice notes field.
help.why: It helps the sequence sound consistent with the existing brand.
help.example: Good example: Calm, practical, slightly informal, and never overly enthusiastic.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Sender perspective
variables.hint: Choose whether the emails speak as a person, founder, team, or company.

help.what: Optional context for the sender perspective field.
help.why: Pronouns, sign-offs, and relationship language depend on the sender perspective.
help.example: Good example: Founder
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Email length
variables.hint: Controls the approximate body length while preserving mobile readability.

help.what: Optional context for the email length field.
help.why: Some sequences require brief operational emails while others need deeper education.
help.example: Good example: Medium
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Cadence style
variables.hint: Choose the general pacing or let the model recommend one.

help.what: Optional context for the cadence style field.
help.why: The desired frequency affects timing recommendations and pressure level.
help.example: Good example: Balanced
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Timing constraints
variables.placeholder: Do not send on weekends; final email must arrive before 12 October.
variables.hint: Add real scheduling restrictions, dates, or automation requirements.

help.what: Optional context for the timing constraints field.
help.why: The model cannot infer business-specific scheduling limitations.
help.example: Good example: Do not send on weekends; final email must arrive before 12 October.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Emoji use
variables.hint: Controls whether emojis may appear in subjects or bodies.

help.what: Optional context for the emoji use field.
help.why: Emoji use is a visible brand and audience preference.
help.example: Good example: Minimal
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Formatting preferences
variables.hint: Choose useful presentation preferences without specifying technical HTML. Current form controls do not support checkboxes for this template yet, so list the relevant options separated by commas.

help.what: List one or more relevant options separated by commas. This preserves the prompt variable while using the current textarea control.
help.why: Formatting affects readability and implementation in the user’s email platform.
help.example: Good example: Short paragraphs, P.S. allowed
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Regulated topic
variables.hint: Choose a category only when the sequence touches a regulated or high-stakes subject.

help.what: Optional context for the regulated topic field.
help.why: High-stakes topics require more cautious language and verification.
help.example: Good example: None
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Relevant jurisdiction
variables.placeholder: European Union, California, United Kingdom, or Ukraine.
variables.hint: Enter the country, region, or legal market relevant to the sequence.

help.what: Optional context for the relevant jurisdiction field.
help.why: Regulated wording and required verification may depend on location.
help.example: Good example: European Union, California, United Kingdom, or Ukraine.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Restrictions
variables.placeholder: Claims to avoid, prohibited terms, required wording, or sensitive topics.
variables.hint: Combine all brand, legal, and editorial restrictions here.

help.what: Optional context for the restrictions field.
help.why: User-specific restrictions cannot be reliably inferred from general best practices.
help.example: Good example: Claims to avoid, prohibited terms, required wording, or sensitive topics.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Output mode
variables.hint: Choose whether you need full copy, strategy, or additional subject-line testing.

help.what: Optional context for the output mode field.
help.why: It controls the amount and type of content generated without adding several separate toggles.
help.example: Good example: Complete package
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Include plain-text versions
variables.hint: Add simplified plain-text copies for systems that do not use formatted email. Choose Yes or No.

help.what: Choose whether the generated package should include separate plain-text email versions.
help.why: Some email platforms and accessibility workflows require a separate plain-text version.
help.example: Good example: Yes
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.

variables.label: Additional context
variables.placeholder: Add any final context that does not fit the fields above.
variables.hint: Use this only for genuinely useful extra requirements.

help.what: Optional context for the additional context field.
help.why: It handles uncommon but relevant information without creating multiple low-value fields.
help.example: Good example: Add any final context that does not fit the fields above.
help.avoid: Avoid guessed details, unsupported claims, or extra requirements that do not apply to this sequence.
